---
title: 'Homework 6'
author: "Frank Marino"
date: 'March 1, 2026'
output:
  html_document: 
    code_download: true
    fig_caption: true
    df_print: kable
    theme: lumen
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: null
    toc_depth: 4
---

## Load Libraries
```{r, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
# Load any R Packages you may need
rm(list = ls())
library(mosaic)
library(tidyverse)
library(treemap)
library(devtools)
install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")
library(d3treeR)
library(dplyr)
```

## Load Data
The following exercises use the `airbnb_boston.csv` data set. Download the .csv file from our Canvas course and load the file into R to complete this assignment. Call this data `airbnb_boston`. Omit null values and then pare down the data set to only include the columns of `price`, `neighbourhood`, and `property_type`.

This assignment uses the `treemap` and `d3Tree` packages. 

```{r, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
# Load Data into R
airbnb_boston <- read_csv("airbnb_boston.csv")
airbnb_boston = airbnb_boston %>%
  na.omit()
airbnb_boston = airbnb_boston %>%
  select(price, neighbourhood, property_type)
```

## Exercise 1
In this exercise, you will focus on creating Tree Maps in R/RStudio.

### (a) 
Using `treemap()`, create a tree map that groups the `airbnb_boston` data by Property Type -> Neighborhood -> Price. This tree map should be static. Save this tree map in a named variable `airbnb_boston_treemap`.

```{r}
airbnb_boston_treemap <- treemap(
  airbnb_boston,
  index = c("property_type", "neighbourhood"),
  vSize = "price",
  type = "index",
  palette = "Set3",
  title = "Airbnb Boston Treemap"
)
```


### (b) 
Using `d3tree()`, convert your static tree map into an interactive tree map.

```{r}
d3tree(airbnb_boston_treemap, rootname = "Airbnb Boston Interactive Treemap")
```

* * *

## Exercise 2
In this exercise, you will focus on creating Tree Maps in Canva.

### (a)
Using the loaded `airbnb_boston` data, you will filter the data set to include eight neighborhoods. Using the code chunk below, filter the `airbnb_boston` data set to eight of the neighborhoods and call this filtered data set `airbnb_boston_filtered`:

:::: {style="display: flex;"}

::: {}
- Allston
- Back Bay
- Bay Village
- Beacon Hill
- Brighton
- Charlestown
- Chinatown
- Dorchester
- Downtown
:::

::: {}
- East Boston
- Fenway
- Hyde Park
- Jamaica Plain
- Leather District
- Longwood Medical Area
- Mattapan
- Mission Hill
- North End
:::

::: {}
- Roslindale
- Roxbury
- South Boston
- South Boston Waterfront
- South End
- West End
- West Roxbury
:::

::::

### Filter Data
*NOTE: Be sure to match exactly the spelling and case sensitivity when selecting categories or your data set might not filter properly!*
```{r, warning=FALSE}
keep_neigh <- c(
  "East Boston", "Back Bay", "South Boston", "North End",
  "Fenway", "Chinatown", "Beacon Hill", "Downtown"
)

airbnb_boston_filtered <- airbnb_boston %>%
  filter(neighbourhood %in% keep_neigh)
```

###(b)
You are tasked with creating an interactive tree map using the template found in Canva. Your tree map should be the same variables as Exercise 1: Property Type -> Neighborhood -> Price. 
1. Create a Canva Presentation with **ONE** slide.
2. Click on **ELEMENTS** on the left-hand menu.
3. Scroll down and click on *See all* next to the header for **CHARTS**
4. Under **INTERACTIVE CHARTS**, click on the second option for **Treemap Chart**.

You may need to perform some analysis in R in order to get the data required to create the tree map. Perform any additional R analysis here:

### R Analysis
*Perform all your R analysis here. Use comments to break-out the different elements you are going to include.*
```{r}
airbnb_boston_summary <- airbnb_boston_filtered %>%
  group_by(neighbourhood, property_type) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_listings = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(neighbourhood, desc(mean_price))

airbnb_boston_summary
```
```{r}
canva_treemap_data <- airbnb_boston_summary %>%
  transmute(
    PropertyType = property_type,
    Neighbourhood = neighbourhood,
    Value = mean_price
  )

write_csv(canva_treemap_data, "canva.csv")
```


```{r}
airbnb_boston_tableau <- airbnb_boston %>%
  group_by(neighbourhood, property_type) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_listings = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(neighbourhood, desc(mean_price))
write_csv(airbnb_boston_tableau, "tableau_data.csv")
```


### (b)

Embed your Canva Presentation Here: 


<div style="position: relative; width: 100%; height: 0; padding-top: 56.2500%;
 padding-bottom: 0; box-shadow: 0 2px 8px 0 rgba(63,69,81,0.16); margin-top: 1.6em; margin-bottom: 0.9em; overflow: hidden;
 border-radius: 8px; will-change: transform;">
  <iframe loading="lazy" style="position: absolute; width: 100%; height: 100%; top: 0; left: 0; border: none; padding: 0;margin: 0;"
    src="https://www.canva.com/design/DAHCiUL2qZs/OGXl1PtabivxWlzgsFeh6Q/view?embed" allowfullscreen="allowfullscreen" allow="fullscreen" data-external="1"> 
  </iframe>
</div>
<a href="https:&#x2F;&#x2F;www.canva.com&#x2F;design&#x2F;DAHCiUL2qZs&#x2F;OGXl1PtabivxWlzgsFeh6Q&#x2F;view?utm_content=DAHCiUL2qZs&amp;utm_campaign=designshare&amp;utm_medium=embeds&amp;utm_source=link" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Airbnb Boston Interactive Treetop</a> by Frank Marino





### (c)
Trouble embedding? No worries! Provide your Canva link here as a back-up:

URL Link to Canva Site: [Your Name - Homework 6](**COPY AND PASTE YOUR CANVA DESIGN URL HERE**)

* * * 

## Exercise 3
In this exercise, you will focus on creating Tree Maps in Tableau. The last exercise will use the `airbnb_boston.csv` data set and Tableau. 

### (a)
You are tasked with creating a Tableau Story that has **ONLY** two points: 

1. Story Point 1: A home page dashboard panel with at least 2 visualizations for potential travelers to *view* **AND** *interact* with to make a decision on where they should stay when they visit Boston.

2. Story Point 2: A **tree map** that uses the identical variables above: property_type,neighborhood, and color coded by price.


### (b)
After you create your Tableau story, save your work to Tableau Public and then embed your code below directly into your HTML page. **Be sure to add a lot of blank space as the embed needs space to render this as HTML code.**
 
 

**Copy your Tableau Embed Code Here**

<div class='tableauPlaceholder' id='viz1772413934995' style='position: relative'><noscript><a href='#'><img alt='Story 1 ' src='https:&#47;&#47;public.tableau.com&#47;static&#47;images&#47;as&#47;assign6_17723680114960&#47;Story1&#47;1_rss.png' style='border: none' /></a></noscript><object class='tableauViz'  style='display:none;'><param name='host_url' value='https%3A%2F%2Fpublic.tableau.com%2F' /> <param name='embed_code_version' value='3' /> <param name='site_root' value='' /><param name='name' value='assign6_17723680114960&#47;Story1' /><param name='tabs' value='no' /><param name='toolbar' value='yes' /><param name='static_image' value='https:&#47;&#47;public.tableau.com&#47;static&#47;images&#47;as&#47;assign6_17723680114960&#47;Story1&#47;1.png' /> <param name='animate_transition' value='yes' /><param name='display_static_image' value='yes' /><param name='display_spinner' value='yes' /><param name='display_overlay' value='yes' /><param name='display_count' value='yes' /><param name='language' value='en-US' /></object></div>

```{js, embedcode, echo = FALSE}
var divElement = document.getElementById('viz1772413934995');
var vizElement = divElement.getElementsByTagName('object')[0];                    vizElement.style.width='100%';vizElement.style.height=(divElement.offsetWidth*0.75)+'px';
var scriptElement = document.createElement('script'); 
scriptElement.src = 'https://public.tableau.com/javascripts/api/viz_v1.js';                    vizElement.parentNode.insertBefore(scriptElement, vizElement);
```
 

### (c)
Trouble embedding? No worries! Provide your Tableau Public Dashboard link here as a back-up:

URL Link to Tableau Dashboard: [Frank Marino- Homework 6](**COPY AND PASTE YOUR TABLEAU DASHBOARD URL HERE**)

* * *

## All done!

Knit the completed R Markdown file as a HTML document (click the "Knit" button at the top of the script editor window) and upload it to the submission portal on Canvas.
